title: How to Handle "Can We Negotiate the Rent?"
dek: Every rental agent hears it: "Can we do anything about the price?" The wrong answer costs you either the client's trust or the building's. Here's the answer that protects both.
heroAlt: How to Handle "Can We Negotiate the Rent?"

blocks.html: Large professionally managed buildings price rent through revenue management systems, and base rent is rarely negotiable. Pretending otherwise, "let me see what I can do about that number," sets up a failure you'll own. Honesty is the only durable move.

"Base rent in this building is set by their revenue system, so that number is what it is, and honestly that consistency is part of why the building holds its quality. Where there IS flexibility sometimes: move-in date, look-and-lease specials, parking, or a longer lease term for a better rate. Want me to find out what's available on this unit? That's exactly the kind of thing my relationship with this office is for."

blocks.text: Don't negotiate the number. Expand the deal.

blocks.html: You told the truth, which luxury clients respect. You redirected from an immovable number to genuinely movable value: concessions, timing, parking, term length. And the closing line quietly states the entire case for using an agent: access. You can get answers and options a stranger can't.

blocks.text: Know the levers before they ask

blocks.html: This is Building Bible work. Know each building's current specials, term-length pricing, and concession patterns BEFORE the tour, and you can often answer the negotiation question preemptively: "One thing worth knowing about this building: they're running six weeks free on 14-month terms right now." A concern answered before it's voiced feels like insight.
The full negotiation and concession playbook is in The Luxury Leasing Playbook, twenty dollars. Better agents win.

blocks.lead: The field manual, day by day.
